agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and business value proposition.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle — intersects with Compliance, Customer Reporting, Dispute Resolution, and Financial Audit bounded contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete audit trail of a delivery: pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, recipient confirmation, timestamps, and associated metadata. This is a legal document, not just operational data."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient — signature, photo, GPS coordinates, timestamp. Required for dispute resolution and has legal evidentiary value."},{"term":"Data Retention Period","definition":"The minimum duration data must be preserved to satisfy legal, regulatory, contractual, and business obligations. Not a storage preference — a compliance requirement."},{"term":"Consignment Note","definition":"Under CMR Convention, the transport document that must be retained as evidence of the contract of carriage and its execution."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of platform delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations — making the platform a data processor with retention obligations."},{"term":"Statute of Limitations Window","definition":"The legal period during which claims can be brought. Deleting evidence within this window exposes the company to indefensible litigation."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records in the UK must be retained for a minimum of 6 years to cover the statute of limitations for contract and negligence claims under the Limitation Act 1980.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal constraint, not a business policy. Violating it means destroying evidence within the litigation window, exposing the company to adverse inferences in court and regulatory penalties.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record associated with a UK transaction may be deleted before 6 years from the delivery date."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records involving cross-border EU shipments must be retained for the period required by the CMR Convention (minimum 1 year for claims, but typically 3-6 years depending on member state implementation) and applicable national law.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The CMR Convention governs international carriage of goods by road. Retention periods are set by international treaty and national law, not by company policy.","testable_assertion":"No cross-border delivery record may be deleted before the longest applicable statute of limitations in the origin and destination countries."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"VAT-related delivery records in the EU/UK must be retained for a minimum of 6-10 years depending on jurisdiction (e.g., 10 years in France, 7 years in Germany, 6 years in UK) as proof of supply and zero-rating for cross-border transactions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit and demand proof of delivery for VAT purposes. Destroying records within the mandatory tax retention period constitutes a tax compliance violation.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record that serves as VAT evidence may be deleted before the applicable tax retention period in the relevant jurisdiction."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Delivery records that are subject to active disputes, legal holds, or ongoing investigations must never be auto-deleted regardless of any retention policy.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence subject to legal hold is spoliation — a criminal offense in many jurisdictions and grounds for severe sanctions in litigation.","testable_assertion":"The auto-deletion process must check for legal holds, active disputes, and investigations, and must skip any record flagged by any of these."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"B2B customers who contractually rely on the platform for compliance reporting data must have access to delivery records for the duration specified in their service agreements.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting data that customers contractually depend on for their own compliance reporting is a breach of contract and could cause downstream regulatory violations for those customers.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record may be deleted if it falls within the contractual data availability period of any B2B customer agreement."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"GDPR data minimization does not override sector-specific retention obligations. Data must be retained when there is a lawful basis (legal obligation, legitimate interest for litigation defense).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR Article 17(3)(b) and (e) explicitly exempt data required for legal claims and legal obligations from the right to erasure. Misapplying GDPR to delete legally required records is itself a compliance failure.","testable_assertion":"The retention policy must document the lawful basis for each retention period category and must not delete records where a legal obligation or litigation defense basis exists."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A B2B customer is audited by their national tax authority 4 years after a delivery and requests POD data to prove zero-rated VAT on cross-border shipments.","expected_behavior":"The delivery record and POD must be available. Under the 90-day policy, this data would have been destroyed 3+ years ago, making the B2B customer unable to satisfy the audit — exposing both the customer and the platform to liability."},{"scenario":"A consumer files a lawsuit for damaged goods 5 years after delivery (within UK limitation period).","expected_behavior":"The platform must produce the delivery record, POD, and chain of custody evidence. Under the 90-day policy, this evidence is destroyed, and the platform faces adverse inference — the court may assume the evidence would have been unfavorable."},{"scenario":"A cross-border shipment from France to Germany requires POD retention for 10 years under French tax law.","expected_behavior":"The record must be retained for 10 years. The 90-day policy would violate French tax retention requirements."},{"scenario":"A delivery record is 85 days old and a legal hold is placed due to a newly filed dispute.","expected_behavior":"The record must be exempted from any auto-deletion. A race condition between the 90-day TTL and legal hold application could result in evidence spoliation."},{"scenario":"B2B customer's SLA guarantees 5 years of delivery data access for their compliance reporting.","expected_behavior":"The platform must honor the contractual retention period. Auto-deleting at 90 days would be a material breach of contract."},{"scenario":"GDPR data subject access request (DSAR) is received for a delivery from 2 years ago.","expected_behavior":"If data was lawfully retained (legal obligation basis), it must be producible. If it was deleted under the 90-day policy despite a legal retention obligation existing, the platform has both destroyed legally required records AND cannot fulfill the DSAR accurately."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Potential exposure includes: (1) Tax penalties across multiple EU jurisdictions for failure to retain VAT evidence — fines can reach 40-100% of the disputed tax amount in France. (2) Litigation losses from inability to defend delivery disputes — the platform loses every case where it cannot produce POD evidence. (3) B2B contract breach damages and customer churn — logistics companies cannot use a platform that destroys their compliance data. (4) Regulatory fines from data protection authorities if records are deleted in violation of legal obligations (paradoxically, misapplying GDPR). Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single adverse tax audit or lost litigation.","operational":"HIGH. Implementing jurisdiction-aware retention is complex but necessary. A blanket 90-day policy is operationally simple but legally non-compliant. The correct approach is tiered retention by jurisdiction and record type, which is more complex to build but is the only compliant path. Additionally, any data already deleted cannot be recovered — making this change partially irreversible if deployed prematurely.","user":"HIGH. B2C consumers lose access to their delivery history after 90 days — no ability to reference past deliveries for returns, warranties, or disputes. B2B customers lose the core compliance reporting capability that may be the reason they chose this platform. Trust erosion is significant: a logistics platform that cannot maintain delivery records signals operational immaturity.","risk":"CRITICAL. This change creates multi-jurisdictional legal exposure across tax, contract, litigation, and data protection law simultaneously. The risk is asymmetric: storage costs saved are measurable and modest; potential losses from legal non-compliance are unpredictable and potentially existential for the business. The framing as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' is itself a risk indicator — it suggests the legal and compliance implications have not been evaluated.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Cannot fulfill their own compliance and audit obligations. May face regulatory penalties. Will likely churn or demand contractual remedies. Potential class-action exposure if many customers are affected simultaneously."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Lose delivery history, proof of delivery for warranty claims, and evidence for disputes. Reduced trust and satisfaction."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"Unable to defend VAT audits across EU/UK jurisdictions. Cross-border zero-rating claims become unsubstantiable."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"Destroyed litigation defense evidence. Spoliation exposure. Unable to respond to legal holds if data is already gone."},{"group":"Engineering / Infrastructure","impact":"The stated beneficiary of this change. Storage cost reduction is real but must be achieved through compliant means — e.g., tiered storage, compression, archival to cold storage — not deletion."},{"group":"Executive Leadership","impact":"Bears ultimate responsibility for regulatory compliance. This change, if approved without legal review, creates personal liability exposure for directors under EU regulations."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Confidence is critically low because: (1) The proposal contains a fundamental contradiction — it claims 'no business logic changes' while proposing to delete legally required records in regulated EU/UK markets. (2) There is no indication that Legal, Compliance, Tax, or B2B customer-facing teams have been consulted. (3) The actual legal retention requirements per jurisdiction have not been specified or mapped. (4) B2B contract terms regarding data availability are unknown. (5) The change may be partially irreversible if deployed — deleted records cannot be recovered.","high_confidence_areas":["UK limitation period requires ~6 years for contract claims","EU VAT retention requirements exceed 90 days in every member state","B2B customers relying on data for compliance reporting would be materially harmed","GDPR does not require deletion of data with a legal obligation basis","This is categorically not just an infrastructure optimization"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention requirements per EU member state the platform operates in","Specific B2B contract terms regarding data availability","Whether any delivery records can safely be deleted at 90 days (e.g., anonymized operational metrics)","Current storage costs and whether cheaper alternatives like cold storage have been evaluated","Whether personal data can be separated from legally required delivery records to satisfy both GDPR minimization and legal retention"],"assumptions":["The platform handles physical goods delivery, not just digital logistics","Delivery records contain POD, timestamps, and transaction data usable for VAT purposes","B2B customers have contractual expectations of multi-year data availability","The platform has not yet obtained legal counsel sign-off on this change"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes' but in reality it eliminates legally mandated data retention in regulated EU/UK markets. This is a material compliance change, not an infrastructure optimization. The framing itself is dangerous because it bypasses the legal and compliance review that would normally gate such a change.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","B2B Customers","Finance/Tax Team","Executive Leadership","B2C Consumers"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day blanket deletion policy entirely and implement jurisdiction-aware tiered retention with archival to cold storage for cost savings","B: Engage Legal and Compliance to define minimum retention periods per jurisdiction and record type before any engineering work begins","C: Separate personal data from delivery records — anonymize personal data per GDPR timelines while retaining non-personal delivery evidence for legal retention periods"],"recommendation":"Option B first, then A and C in combination. No engineering work should begin until Legal has signed off on minimum retention periods per jurisdiction. Storage cost reduction should be pursued through tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) and compression — not deletion. This must be re-classified from an infrastructure ticket to a legal/compliance initiative."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Critical information is missing: (1) Which specific EU member states does the platform operate in? Each has different retention requirements. (2) What do B2B customer contracts specify about data availability? (3) Has Legal/Compliance reviewed this proposal? (4) What is the actual storage cost being targeted for reduction? (5) Has tiered/cold storage been evaluated as an alternative to deletion?","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering Team","Legal/Compliance Team","B2B Customers"],"options":["Proceed with analysis using worst-case assumptions per jurisdiction","Pause until Legal provides jurisdiction-specific retention schedule"],"recommendation":"Pause all engineering work. Obtain a jurisdiction-specific retention schedule from Legal before defining any acceptance criteria. The cost of getting this wrong vastly exceeds the cost of a brief delay."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Proposing 90-day auto-deletion of delivery records in EU/UK markets where legal retention requirements range from 6-10 years depending on jurisdiction and record type.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3"},{"violation":"Framing a compliance-critical change as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' bypasses required legal and compliance review gates.","business_rule_broken":"All BRs — the framing prevents proper evaluation of any business rule impact."},{"violation":"No consideration of B2B contractual obligations for data availability in compliance reporting.","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"No legal hold exemption mechanism mentioned in the proposal.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"No jurisdiction-aware retention strategy — a blanket 90-day policy cannot be compliant across multiple EU member states with different retention requirements.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2, BR-3"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and business value propositio
